Chapter 7: Of the second outing of our good knight D.
Don Quixote of La Mancha

While this was happening, Don Quixote began to shout, saying: "Here, here, brave knights! Here it is necessary to show the strength of your valiant arms, for the courtiers are taking the best of the tournament."
Because of the attention paid to this noise and clamor, the scrutiny of the remaining books was not carried forward; thus it is believed that they went into the fire unseen and unheard—the Carolea and León de España, along with the Deeds of the Emperor composed by Don Luis de Avila, which undoubtedly must have been among those remaining, and perhaps, had the priest seen them, they would not have suffered such a rigorous sentence.
When they reached Don Quixote, he was already out of bed and continuing with his shouting and delusions, striking out and slashing in all directions, as wide awake as if he had never slept.
They embraced him and by force returned him to his bed; and after he had calmed down a little, turning to speak with the priest, he said: truly, Lord Archbishop Turpin, it is a great shame for those of us called the twelve Peers to leave the victory of this tournament so abruptly to the courtly knights, when we adventurers had won the honor during the three preceding days.
Be silent, your honor, my dear friend, said the priest, for God will be pleased to change your luck, and what is lost today may be gained tomorrow; and let your honor attend to his health for now, as it seems to me that he must be excessively tired, if not badly wounded.
"Wounded, no," said Don Quixote; "but beaten and broken, there is no doubt of it, for that bastard Don Roldan has pummeled me with the trunk of an oak tree, and all out of envy, because he sees that I alone am the opposite of his bravery; but I would not call myself Reinaldos de Montalbán if, upon rising from this bed, I do not make him pay for it, despite all his enchantments; and for now, bring me something to eat, for I know that is what will be most useful to me, and leave the matter of my revenge in my hands."
They did so, gave him something to eat, and he fell asleep again, while they were amazed at his madness.

That night, the mistress burned and consumed as many books as there were in the courtyard and throughout the entire house; and such must have burned that deserved to be kept in perpetual archives; but their fate and the laziness of the examiner did not permit it, and thus the proverb was fulfilled in them, that sometimes the just pay for the sinners.
One of the remedies that the priest and the barber suggested at the time for their friend's ailment was to wall up his book room, so that when he woke up he would not find them (perhaps by removing the cause, the effect would cease), and that they should say a sorcerer had taken them, along with the room and everything.
And so it was done with great haste.

Two days later Don Quixote rose, and the first thing he did was go to see his books; and as he could not find the room where he had left them, he went from one place to another searching for them.
He would go to where the door used to be and feel for it with his hands, turning and rolling his eyes without saying a word; but after a good while, he asked his mistress where the room with his books was located.
The mistress, who had already been well warned as to how she should respond, said to him: "What room, or what is your grace looking for? There are no longer any rooms or books in this house, because the devil himself took it all."
It was not the devil, replied the niece, but an enchanter who came upon a cloud one night after the day your honor departed from here; and dismounting from a serpent on which he rode as a knight, he entered the room. I do not know what he did inside, for after a short while he flew out through the roof, leaving the house full of smoke; and when we decided to look at what he had left behind, we saw neither books nor any room. Only the housekeeper and I remember very well that, as that evil old man was departing, he said in a loud voice that, due to a secret enmity he held toward the owner of those books and that room, he left damage in that house that would be seen later; he also said that his name was the sage Muñatón.
"Friston would say," said Don Quixote.
"I don't know," the mistress replied, "if his name was Freston or Friton; I only know that his name ended in -ton."
That is so, said Don Quixote, for that is an enchanting sorcerer, my great enemy, who bears a grudge against me because he knows, through his arts and letters, that as time goes by, I am destined to fight in single combat with a knight whom he favors, and that I shall defeat him without him

lest I may hinder him, and for this reason he tries to cause me every possible annoyance; yet I tell him that he will be unable to contradict or avoid that which is ordained by heaven.
"Who doubts that?" said the niece.
But what drives your honor, my dear uncle, into these quarrels? Would it not be better to remain peacefully at home, rather than wandering the world in search of something unnecessary, without considering that many go for wool and come back shorn? Oh, my niece, replied Don Quijote, how wrong you are in your assessment! Before I am shorn, I shall pluck out the beards of all those who imagine they can touch a single hair on my head.
Neither of them wanted to argue with him further, for they saw that his anger was flaring up.
It happened, then, that he remained at home for fifteen days in great tranquility, showing no sign of wanting to follow up on his first whims; during those days he spent time telling most amusing tales to his two companions, the priest and the barber, claiming that what the world most needed were knights-errant, and that he would be the one to revive the tradition of knight-errantry.
The priest sometimes contradicted him and at other times agreed, for if he did not maintain this artifice, it was impossible to get anywhere with him.
During this time, Don Quixote made a request to a neighboring farmer, a man of good standing (if such a title can be given to one who is poor), but with very little salt in his head.
In resolution, he spoke to him so much, persuaded and promised him so much, that the poor peasant determined to go with him and serve as his squire.
Don Quixote told him, among other things, to prepare himself to go with him willingly, for it might happen that they would encounter an adventure in which he would win—as easily as one might brush away a few straws—some island, and leave him as its governor.
With these and other such promises, Sancho Panza (for so the laborer was called) left his wife and children, and established himself as the squire of his neighbor.
Don Quixote then gave orders to seek out money; and by selling one thing, pawning another, and underselling them all, he gathered a reasonable amount.
He also provided himself with a buckler that he borrowed from a friend, and after repairing his broken helmet as best he could, he informed his squire Sancho of the day and hour he intended to set out, so that he might provide himself with whatever he saw he needed most; above all, he charged him to bring saddlebags.
He said that he would indeed bring it, and that he also intended to bring a donkey that he had which was very good, because he was not accustomed to walking much on foot.
Don Quixote paused for a moment regarding the donkey, wondering if he could recall whether any knight-errant had ever brought a squire on an ass; but none occurred to him; nevertheless, he decided to take him along, with the intention of providing him with more honorable cavalry as soon as the opportunity arose, by taking the horse from the first discourteous knight he encountered.
He provided himself with shirts and other things he could, according to the advice the innkeeper had given him.

All this having been done and fulfilled, without Sancho saying goodbye to his children and wife, nor Don Quixote to his mistress and niece, one night they left the village without anyone seeing them, and on that journey they traveled so far that by dawn they felt certain that they would not be found even if they were searched for.
Sancho Panza rode his donkey like a patriarch, with his saddlebags and his wineskin, and with a great longing to already see himself as governor of the island that his master had promised him.
Don Quixote decided to take the same route and path that he had previously taken on his first journey, which was through the Field of Montiel; along this way he walked with less hardship than last time, because it being the morning hour and the sun's rays striking them obliquely, they did not fatigue them.
To this Sancho Panza said to his master: look, your honor, sir knight-errant, do not forget what you have promised me regarding the island, for I shall know how to govern it, no matter how large it may be.
To which Don Quixote replied: you must know, friend Sancho Panza, that it was a very common custom among the knights-errant of old to appoint their squires as governors of the islands or kingdoms they conquered; and I am determined that, through me, such a grateful custom shall not be lacking; indeed, I intend to surpass them in it, for they sometimes, and perhaps most often, waited until their squires were old, and only after they had grown weary of serving, and of enduring bad days and worse nights, did they grant them some title of count; or at least that of marquess of some valley or province of more or less importance; but if you live and I live, it could well be that in fewer than six days I may win such a kingdom, that

had others adhere to him, who would come prepared to crown you king of one of them.
And do not think it too much, for such things and cases befall these gentlemen in ways so never before seen nor imagined, that I could easily give you even more than what I promise you.
In that way, Sancho Panza replied, if I were king by some miracle of those your honor speaks of, at the very least Juana Gutiérrez, my wife, would become queen and my children princes.
"And who doubts it?" replied Don Quixote.
"I doubt it," replied Sancho Panza, "for I believe that even if God were to rain kingdoms upon the earth, none would sit well on the head of Mari Gutiérrez."
Know, sir, that she is not worth two maravedis as a queen; countess would suit her better, or even "God and help."
"Commit it to God, Sancho," replied Don Quixote, "for He will give him what best suits him; but do not humble your spirit so much as to be content with less than being a governor."
"I shall not, my lord," replied Sancho, "and all the more so having such a distinguished master in your grace, who will know how to give me everything that is fitting and that I can bear."

Capítulo 7: De la segunda salida de nuestro buen caballero D. 
Quijote de la Mancha

Estando en esto, comenzó a dar voces Don Quijote, diciendo: aquí, aquí, valerosos caballeros, aquí es menester mostrar la fuerza de vuestros valerosos brazos, que los cortesanos llevan lo mejor del torneo. 
Por acudir a este ruido y estruendo no se pasó adelante con el escrutinio de los demás libros que quedaban, y así se cree que fueron al fuego sin ser vistos ni oídos, la Carolea y León de España, con los Hechos del emperador, compuestos por don Luis de Avila, que sin duda debían de estar entre los que quedaban, y quizá, si el cura los viera, no pasaran por tan rigurosa sentencia. 
Cuando llegaron a Don Quijote, ya él estaba levantado de la cama, y proseguía en sus voces y en sus desatinos, dando cuchilladas y reveses a todas partes, estando tan despierto como si nunca hubiera dormido. 
Abrazáronse con él, y por fuerza le volvieron al lecho; y después que hubo sosegado un poco, volviéndose a hablar con el cura, le dijo: por cierto, señor Arzobispo Turpin, que es gran mengua de los que nos llamamos doce Pares dejar tan sin más ni más llevar la victoria de este torneo a los caballeros cortesanos, habiendo nosotros los aventureros ganado el prez, en los tres días antecedentes. 
Calle vuestra merced, señor compadre, dijo el cura, que Dios será servido que la suerte se mude, y que lo que hoy se pierde se gane mañaa; y atienda vuestra merced a su salud por ahora, que me parece que debe de estar demasiadamente cansado, si ya no es que está mal ferido. 
Ferido no, dijo Don Quijote; pero molido y quebrantado no hay duda en ello, porque aquel astardo de don Roldán me ha molido a palos con el tronco de una encina, y todo de envidia, porque ve que yo solo soy el opuesto de sus valentías; mas no me llamaría yo Reinaldos de Montalbán, si en levantándome de este lecho no me lo pagare, a pesar de todos sus encantamientos; y por ahora tráigame de yantar, que sé que es lo que más me hará al caso, y quédese lo del vengarme a mi cargo. 
Hiciéronlo así, diéronle de comer, y quedóse otra vez dormido, y ellos admirados de su locura.

Aquella noche quemó y abrasó el ama cuantos libros había en el corral y en toda la casa, y tales debieron de arder, que merecían guardarse en perpetuos archivos; mas no lo permitió su suerte y la pereza del escrutinador, y así se cumplió el refrán en ellos, de que pagan a veces justos por pecadores. 
Uno de los remedios que el cura y el barbero dieron por entonces para el mal de su amigo, fue que le murasen y tapiasen el aposento de los libros, porque cuando se levantase no los hallase (quizá quitando la causa cesaría el efecto), y que dijesen que uun encantador se los había llevado, y el aposento y todo. 
Y así fue hecho con mucha presteza.

De allí a dos días se levantó Don Quijote, y lo primero que hizo fue ir a ver sus libros; y como no hallaba el aposento donde le había dejado, andaba de una a otra parte buscándole. 
Llegaba adonde solía tener la puerta, y tentábala con las manos, y volvía y revolvía los ojos sin decir palabra; pero al cabo de una buena pieza, preguntó a su ama que hacía qué parte estaba el aposento de sus libros. 
El ama, que ya estaba bien advertida de lo que había de responder, le dijo: ¿qué aposento, o qué anda buscando vuestra merced? Ya no hay aposento ni libros en esta casa porque todo se lo llevó el mismo diablo. 
No era el diablo, replicó la sobrina, sino un encantador que vino sobre una nube una noche después del día que vuestra merced de aquí se partió, y apeándose de una sierpe en que venía caballero, entró en el aposento; y no sé lo que hizo dentro, que a cabo de poca pieza salió volando por el tejado, y dejó la casa llena de humo; y cuando acordamos a mirar lo que dejaba hecho, no vimos libros ni aposento alguno; sólo se nos acuerda muy bien a mí y al ama, que al tiempo de partirse aquel mal viejo, dijo en altas voces, que por enemistad secreta que tenía al dueño de aquellos libros y aposento, dejaba hecho el daño en aquella casa que después se vería; dijo también qeu se llamaba el sabio Muñatón. 
Fristón diría, dijo Don Quijote. 
No sé, respondió el ama, si se llamaba Frestón o Fritón; sólo sé que acabó en ton su nombre. 
Así es, dijo Don Quijote, que ese es un sabio encantador, grande enemigo mío, que me tiene ojeriza porque sabe, por sus artes y letras, que tengo de venir, andando los tiempos, a pelear en singular batalla con un caballero a quien él favorece, y le tengo de vencer sin que él

lo pueda estorbar, y por esto procura hacerme todos los sinsabores que puede; y mándole yo, qué mal podrá él contradecir ni evitar lo que por el cielo está ordenado. 
¿Quién duda de eso? dijo la sobrina. 
Pero ¿quién le mete a vuestra merced, señor tío, en esas pendencias? ¿No será mejor estarse pacífico en su casa, y no irse por el mundo a buscar pan de trastrigo, sin considerar que muchos van por lana y vuelven trasquilados? ¡Oh, sobrina mía, respondió Don Quijote, y cuán mal que estás en la cuenta! Primero que a mí me trasquilen, tendré peladas y quitadas las barbas a cuantos imaginaren tocarme en la punta de un solo cabello. 
No quisieron las dos replicarle más, porque vieron que se le encendía la cólera. 
Es, pues, el caso que él estuvo quince días en casa muy sosegado, sin dar muestras de querer secundar sus primeros devaneos, en los cuales días pasó graciosísimos cuentos con sus dos compadres el cura y el barbero, sobre que él decía que la cosa de que más necesidad tenía el mundo era de caballeros andantes, y de que en él se resucitase la caballería andantesca. 
El cura algunas veces le contradecía y otras concedía, porque si no guardaba este artificio, no había poder averiguarse con él. 
En este tiempo solicitó Don Quijote a un labrador vecino suyo, hombre de bien (si es que ese título se puede dar al que es pobre), pero de muy poca sal en la mollera. 
En resolución, tanto le dijo, tanto le persuadió y prometió, que el pobre villano se determinó de salir con él y servirle de escudero. 
Decíale entre otras cosas Don Quijote, que se dispusiese a ir con él de buena gana, porque tal vez le podía suceder aventura que ganase en quítame allá esas pajas, alguna ínsula, y le dejase a él por gobernador de ella. 
Con estas promesas y otras tales, Sancho Panza (que así se llamaba el labrador) dejó su mujer e hijos, y asentó por escudero de su vecino. 
Dió luego Don Quijote orden en buscar dineros; y vendiendo una cosa, y empeñando otra, y malbaratándolas todas, allegó una razonable cantidad. 
Acomodóse asimismo de una rodela que pidió prestada a un su amigo, y pertrechando a su rota celada lo mejor que pudo, avisó a su escudero Sancho del día y la hora que pensaba ponerse en camino, para que él se acomodase de lo que viese que más le era menester; sobre todo, le encargó que llevase alforjas. 
El dijo que sí llevaría, y que asimismo pensaba llevar un asno que tenía muy bueno, porque él no estaba ducho a andar mucho a pie. 
En lo del asno reparó un poco Don Quijote, imaginando si se le acordaba si algún caballero andante había traido escudero caballero asnalmente; pero nunca le vino alguno a la memoria; mas con todo esto, determinó que le llevase, con presupuesto de acomodarle de más honrada caballería en habiendo ocasión para ello, quitándole el caballo al primer descortés caballero que topase. 
Proveyóse de camisas y de las demás cosas que él pudo, conforme al consejo que el ventero le había dado.

Todo lo cual hecho y cumplido, sin despedirse Panza de sus hijos y mujer, ni Don Quijote de su ama y sobrina, una noche se salieron del lugar sin que persona los viese, en la cual caminaron tanto, que al amanecer se tuvieron por seguros de que no los hallarían aunque les buscasen. 
Iba Sancho Panza sobre su jumento como un patriarca, con sus alforjas y su bota, y con mucho deseo de verse ya gobernador de la ínsula que su amo le había prometido. 
Acertó Don Quijote a tomar la misma derrota y camino que el que él había antes tomado en su primer viaje, que fue por el Campo de Montiel, por el cual caminaba con menos pesadumbre que la vez pasada, porque por ser la hora de lamañana y herirles a soslayo los rayos del sol, no les fatigaban. 
Dijo en esto Sancho Panza a su amo: mire vuestra merced, señor caballero andante, que no se le olvide lo que de la ínsula me tiene prometido, que yo la sabré gobernar por grande que sea. 
A lo cual le respondió Don Quijote: has de saber, amigo Sancho Panza, que fue costumbre muy usada de los caballeros andantes antiguos hacer gobernadores a sus escuderos de las ínsulas o reinos que ganaban; y yo tengo determinado de que por mí no falte tan agradecida usanza; antes pienso aventajarme en ella, porque ellos algunas veces, y quizá las más, esperaban a que sus escuderos fuesen viejos, y ya después de hartos de servir, y de llevar malos días y peores noches, les daban algún título de conde; o por lo menos de marqués de algún valle o provincia de poco más o menos; pero si tú vives y yo vivo, bien podría ser que antes de seis días ganase yo tal reino, que

tuviese otros a él adherentes, que viniesen de molde para coronarte por rey de uno de ellos. 
Y no lo tengas a mucho, que cosas y casos acontecen a los tales caballeros, por modos tan nunca vistos ni pensados, que con facilidad te podría dar aún más de lo que te prometo. 
De esa manera, respondió Sancho Panza, si yo fuese rey por algún milagro de los que vuestra merced dice, por lo menos Juana Gutiérrez, mi oislo, vendría a ser reina y mis hijos infantes. 
¿Pues quién lo duda? respondión Don Quijote. 
Yo lo dudo, respondió Sancho Panza, porque tengo para mí que aunque lloviese Dios reinos sobre la tierra, ninguno asentaría bien sobre la cabeza de Mari Gutiérrez. 
Sepa, señor, que no vale dos maravedís para reina; condesa le caerá mejor, y aún Dios y ayuda. 
Encomiéndalo tú a Dios, Sancho, respondió Don Quijote, que él le dará lo que más le conventa; pero no apoques tu ánimo tanto que te vengas a contentar con menos que con ser adelantado. 
No haré, señor mío, respondió Sancho, y más teniendo tan principal amo en vuestra merced, que me sabrá dar todo aquello que me esté bien y yo pueda llevar.
